title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about sleep science and the nature of dreams. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Sleeping Mind
content.subtitle: An article exploring what science has discovered about sleep and dreaming.
content.text:
Sleep is one of the most fundamental activities in the animal kingdom, and yet for most of human history it was treated as little more than a necessary inconvenience — a suspension of real life rather than a process in its own right. That understanding has been transformed in recent decades. Research now shows that far from being passive, the sleeping brain is engaged in a complex programme of maintenance, consolidation, and, some researchers argue, creativity. [31] Sleep is no longer seen as time lost; increasingly, it is understood as the hidden architecture that supports everything we do while we are awake. [/31]

The most extensively studied phase of sleep is REM sleep — rapid eye movement sleep — during which brain activity resembles wakefulness and vivid dreaming typically occurs. [32] During REM sleep, the brain replays and processes emotional experiences from the day, apparently stripping away some of the distress while preserving the factual content of the memory. This has led some researchers to describe REM sleep as 'overnight therapy', a nightly recalibration of the emotional brain. [/32] The theory has attracted clinical interest, since disrupted REM sleep is consistently associated with conditions including anxiety, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder.

The function of dreams themselves remains one of the more contested areas of sleep science. One influential view holds that dreams are not meaningful at all — that they are the byproduct of random neural firing, and that the brain's tendency to impose narrative on them is an automatic pattern-making process with no deeper significance. [33] A competing perspective argues that dreams allow the brain to simulate threatening scenarios, testing responses in a low-risk environment, which would explain the prevalence of chase sequences and other archetypal anxiety dreams. [/33]

Beyond REM sleep, a great deal of important work happens during the deeper stages of non-REM sleep. It is here that the brain is believed to consolidate declarative memories — facts, events, and the kind of explicit knowledge tested in exams. [34] Studies in which participants were taught new material before sleep consistently found superior recall in those who slept for a full night compared to those who were kept awake — evidence that has had obvious implications for the debate about student sleep habits. [/34]

The modern world is conducting what amounts to an involuntary experiment in chronic sleep deprivation. Artificial light has extended the working day; smartphones deliver stimulation until the moment the light goes out; and a culture that equates busyness with productivity has made inadequate sleep a strange kind of status symbol. [35] The consequences, as accumulated research makes clear, extend well beyond tiredness: impaired cognition, weakened immunity, increased risk of a range of serious physical conditions, and a measurably negative effect on mood and emotional regulation. [/35]

Sleep recommendations vary slightly by age group, but most adults require between seven and nine hours per night. [36] Yet surveys consistently show that a significant proportion of adults in developed nations fall substantially short of this — not because they cannot sleep, but because they prioritise other activities in the hours that sleep would otherwise occupy. This, sleep researchers insist, is one of the most modifiable risk factors for both physical and mental health. [/36]

questions.question: The author states that scientists now regard sleep as
options: A) a passive state in which the body recovers from the demands of the day.
B) an essential process that actively supports our waking mental functions.
C) primarily important for physical recovery rather than brain function.
D) something that becomes less important as we get older.
questions.explanation: Sleep is described as 'the hidden architecture that supports everything we do while we are awake', indicating it actively underpins waking function.

questions.question: The phrase 'overnight therapy' refers to the idea that during REM sleep
options: A) the brain creates solutions to problems encountered during the day.
B) emotional memories are processed in a way that reduces their distressing impact.
C) the brain restores damaged neural connections caused by stress.
D) dreams help the brain prepare emotionally for challenges the following day.
questions.explanation: The brain 'replays and processes emotional experiences from the day, apparently stripping away some of the distress while preserving the factual content of the memory'.

questions.question: The view that dreams allow the brain to simulate threatening scenarios suggests that
options: A) nightmares are a sign of underlying psychological problems.
B) dreams are a form of entertainment generated by the sleeping brain.
C) common anxious dream themes may have a protective biological function.
D) the brain confuses real and imagined threats during REM sleep.
questions.explanation: Dreams are described as 'testing responses in a low-risk environment', which 'would explain the prevalence of chase sequences and other archetypal anxiety dreams' — suggesting a protective function.

questions.question: Research comparing students who slept with those who stayed awake after studying shows that
options: A) studying immediately before sleep is more effective than studying during the day.
B) sleep plays an important role in the long-term retention of information.
C) students who stay awake to study are more likely to perform poorly in exams.
D) non-REM sleep is more important for academic performance than REM sleep.
questions.explanation: Those who 'slept for a full night' showed 'superior recall', providing evidence that sleep is important for consolidating information — i.e., for memory retention.

questions.question: The author describes modern sleep deprivation as 'a strange kind of status symbol' because
options: A) wealthy people have access to more distractions that keep them awake.
B) sleeping less has become associated with being productive and hardworking.
C) smartphones make it socially necessary to stay connected at all hours.
D) people who sleep less tend to earn more money in their careers.
questions.explanation: 'A culture that equates busyness with productivity' has made sleeping less appear as evidence of hard work and dedication — hence a form of status symbol.

questions.question: According to sleep researchers, the main reason adults sleep less than recommended is that
options: A) most adults suffer from undiagnosed sleep disorders.
B) the demands of modern working life make adequate sleep impossible.
C) people choose to use potential sleeping time for other activities.
D) most adults do not know how much sleep they actually need.
questions.explanation: The surveys show adults fall short of sleep recommendations 'not because they cannot sleep, but because they prioritise other activities in the hours that sleep would otherwise occupy'.
